title: DuckDB: cast VARCHAR date columns before using date functions
intent_tags: no function matches date_trunc string_literal varchar

dialect_notes.duckdb: date_trunc(), date_diff(), EXTRACT(), strftime() ALL require a DATE or TIMESTAMP argument — not VARCHAR. Always cast VARCHAR date columns first.

template:
SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('month', CAST("Order Date" AS DATE)) AS month,
    SUM(sales) AS total_sales,
    SUM(profit) AS total_profit
FROM main.superstore
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1
when_to_use: DuckDB query involving date_trunc, date_diff, date_part, EXTRACT, strftime on a column whose schema type is VARCHAR, TEXT, or CHARACTER VARYING
monthly/yearly/quarterly aggregation on a date column loaded from CSV that is stored as VARCHAR in DuckDB
any date arithmetic on a DuckDB VARCHAR column (date comparison, interval addition, etc.)
anti_patterns: DATE_TRUNC('month', "Order Date") when "Order Date" is VARCHAR — raises 'No function matches date_trunc(STRING_LITERAL, VARCHAR)'; always cast: DATE_TRUNC('month', CAST("Order Date" AS DATE))
date_diff('day', "Start Date", "End Date") when both are VARCHAR — cast each side: date_diff('day', "Start Date"::DATE, "End Date"::DATE)
EXTRACT(year FROM "Order Date") when "Order Date" is VARCHAR — cast first: EXTRACT(year FROM "Order Date"::DATE)
strftime("Order Date", '%Y-%m') on a VARCHAR column — cast: strftime("Order Date"::TIMESTAMP, '%Y-%m')
using TRY_CAST without checking the date format — if values are not parseable as DATE, TRY_CAST returns NULL silently, producing empty results
notes: Two equivalent cast syntaxes in DuckDB: CAST(col AS DATE) or col::DATE. Both work identically.
For CSV-loaded data with mixed/unknown date formats use strptime(col, 'format_string') to parse before truncating.
Common CSV date formats: '%m/%d/%Y' (US), '%d/%m/%Y' (EU), '%Y-%m-%d' (ISO). Check sample values to confirm.
If the column contains datetime strings (e.g. '2023-01-15 08:30:00'), cast to TIMESTAMP, not DATE: CAST(col AS TIMESTAMP).
date_trunc on a TIMESTAMP returns a TIMESTAMP; on a DATE returns a TIMESTAMP. Both group correctly by month.

title: DuckDB: parse VARCHAR dates with strptime when format is non-ISO

dialect_notes.duckdb: Use strptime(col, format) for non-ISO date strings. ISO dates (YYYY-MM-DD) can be cast directly with ::DATE.

template:
SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('month', strptime("Order Date", '%m/%d/%Y')) AS month,
    SUM(sales) AS total_sales
FROM main.superstore
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1

-- Alternative: TRY_STRPTIME for fault tolerance (returns NULL on unparseable values)
SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('month', TRY_STRPTIME("Order Date", '%m/%d/%Y')) AS month,
    SUM(sales) AS total_sales
FROM main.superstore
WHERE TRY_STRPTIME("Order Date", '%m/%d/%Y') IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1
when_to_use: DuckDB date column stored as VARCHAR in US format MM/DD/YYYY (Superstore, most US Excel exports)
any non-ISO date string that cannot be directly CAST to DATE (CAST fails or produces wrong results)
use TRY_STRPTIME instead of strptime to skip unparseable rows rather than raising an error
anti_patterns: CAST("Order Date" AS DATE) on a MM/DD/YYYY string like '01/15/2023' — DuckDB interprets CAST as ISO (YYYY-MM-DD) and will either error or parse incorrectly; use strptime("Order Date", '%m/%d/%Y') instead
strptime without checking the actual format in sample data — Superstore uses '%m/%d/%Y'; other datasets may use '%d-%m-%Y' or '%Y/%m/%d'
notes: strptime format codes: %Y=4-digit year, %m=2-digit month, %d=2-digit day, %H=hour, %M=minute, %S=second.
Superstore 'Order Date' and 'Ship Date' columns are typically MM/DD/YYYY strings in CSV form.
strptime returns a TIMESTAMP. Use ::DATE to downcast to DATE if you don't need time of day.

title: DuckDB: QUALIFY for row deduplication without a subquery

dialect_notes.duckdb: QUALIFY filters window function results inline — no subquery needed. Only for window functions (ROW_NUMBER, RANK, etc.); use HAVING for aggregate filters.

template:
SELECT *
FROM orders
QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date DESC) = 1
when_to_use: DuckDB: get the latest/first row per group without wrapping in a subquery
deduplicate a table keeping only the most recent record per entity
top-N rows per partition in DuckDB
anti_patterns: using QUALIFY with GROUP BY ALL — DuckDB does not support QUALIFY + GROUP BY ALL together; use explicit GROUP BY columns
using QUALIFY to filter aggregate results (SUM, AVG, COUNT) — QUALIFY is only for window function results; use HAVING for aggregate filters
QUALIFY is DuckDB/Snowflake/Teradata specific — in PostgreSQL or MySQL use a subquery with WHERE rn = 1 instead
notes: QUALIFY is equivalent to: SELECT * FROM (SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (...) AS rn FROM t) WHERE rn = 1 — but cleaner.
Only available in DuckDB, Snowflake, Teradata, and BigQuery. Not standard SQL.
